text:
Nine’s Paris Olympics coverage has been plunged into chaos after newspaper journalists decided to walk off the job in a pay dispute, despite receiving an all-expense paid trip to the French capital.

Nine Entertainment is in crisis mode after it was confirmed staffers across the company’s struggling newspaper division will walk off the job on the eve of the 2024 Paris Olympics.

Staff at Nine’s metro mastheads, including The Age, the Sydney Morning Herald, Australian Financial Review, WAtoday and Brisbane Times, have voted overwhelmingly in favour of strike action from Friday over a pay dispute.

The action includes 20 reporters who have been flown to Paris to cover the Olympics, potentially jeopardising print and digital coverage of Friday’s opening ceremony.

The strike will not affect Nine's TV coverage of the much-anticipated Games.

Nine is the exclusive broadcast rights holder for the 2024 Paris Games and the move will likely have major implications for Australians seeking to access Olympics content.

As the Paris-based journos presumably eat croissants by the seine, a “skeleton staff” will be on deck in Australia to cover the opening ceremony.

The Australian’s media writer Sophie Elsworth called the outcome a “disaster” and confirmed journalists would “down tools” for five days.

“This could not have come at a worst time,” she told Sky News Australia host Steve Price.

It is understood the journalists opted to strike after union representatives and management failed to find a resolution to ongoing salary disputes on Thursday.

One sports reporter working for a rival broadcaster called the outcome a “shame” and pointed out that covering the Olympics was a career goal for many journalists.

“The Olympics is the pinnacle of the sporting world and undoubtedly a high mark in any journalistic career so to see it spoiled is a real shame,” they told SkyNews.com.au.

According to news.com.au, executives offered a 3.5 per cent annual pay rise to staff in a desperate bid to prevent strikes, compared to the 2 per cent which was already in place.

While Nine operates with a diminished workforce in Paris, the rest of Australia’s media has received threatening messages not to report on the games in a way which could “contravene” rights ownership.

The shock strike action comes after a scandal plagued last six months saw a massive 30 per cent wiped off Nine Entertainment’s share price.

Nine's 200-strong Paris Olympics team was also rocked on Wednesday after it was revealed two staffers have been assaulted in a botched robbery attempt while leaving the international broadcast centre in northeast Paris.

Nine is the exclusive broadcaster for the next five Summer and Winter Olympic Games up until the Brisbane Games in 2032, as part of a $305 million deal spearheaded by boss Mike Sneesby.

On Tuesday, as staffers in Sydney and Melbourne battled over the pay disputes, Mr Sneesby was photographed carrying the Olympic torch through the streets of Paris.

The industrial action comes weeks afterMr Sneesby announced more than 200 job cuts, mostly from the newspaper and broadcast divisions, amid plunging advertising revenue.

Ms Elsworth said staffers were “angry” over Mr Sneesby’s photoshoot in Paris on the heels of the job cuts, which slashed head counts at newsrooms nationwide.

“Staff are really angry with what’s been going on in recent weeks, particularly with Mike Sneesby this week running through the French streets with the Olympic torch, they are angry and are walking off the job at 11am,” she said.

title: More than 90 per cent of Nine print journalists walk off job after all-expense paid trip to Paris Olympics | Sky News Australia
description: Nine Entertainment is in crisis mode after it was confirmed staffers across the company’s struggling newspaper division will walk off the job on the eve of the 2024 Paris Olympics.
